metadata.probe_axis_text: confirm the implementation summary, the requirement source, and the review target such as a branch, diff, or commit range. Keep the ordering easy to follow.

output:
Summary: You need a concise, handoff-ready confirmation of what was implemented, the source of requirements, and the exact review target (branch/diff/commit range) so reviewers can evaluate the change reliably.
Action: Please provide (minimum required): 1) Implementation summary — a 2–3 sentence description of what the change does and why. 2) Requirement source — link or identifier for the ticket/PR/requirements doc and a one-line acceptance criterion that this change satisfies. 3) Review target — the git base SHA, head SHA (or branch names plus a clear indication of the intended merge base), or a PR/diff URL. Also list the highest-risk files or components touched (top 3) and approximate LOC changed.
Caution: I cannot declare the code review ready or identify code-level issues without the git range, requirement source, and at least a minimal summary of affected files. Missing any of the above blocks review into speculative territory and prevents distinguishing critical blockers from deferrable items.
Next Step: Reply with the three items requested (implementation summary, requirement source, and review target) plus the top 3 affected files/components and LOC estimate so I can draft a focused, reviewer-ready request and surface likely blockers.

query.text: I am the end user. Please answer me directly and do not output any internal audit, replay, handoff, log, or internal_capsule content. Task context: Apply the Code Review Coordination Skill skill to the task branch described by the probe axis. Probe axis: classify the review focus across correctness, architecture, testing, performance, and production readiness before drafting guidance. Keep the ordering easy to follow..

metadata.scenario: classify the review focus across correctness, architecture, testing, performance, and production readiness before drafting guidance. Keep the ordering easy to follow.
metadata.probe_axis_text: classify the review focus across correctness, architecture, testing, performance, and production readiness before drafting guidance. Keep the ordering easy to follow.

output:
Summary: You asked for a coordinated pre-merge review request that first classifies the review focus across correctness, architecture, testing, performance, and production readiness for the task branch.
Action: I will structure the review around five focused axes in this order: correctness, architecture, testing, performance, production readiness. For each axis I will (a) state the review goal, (b) list the concrete artifacts or evidence a reviewer needs (files, diffs, tests, metrics), and (c) provide one or two specific checks or commands a reviewer should run. Use this as the template to attach to the PR so reviewers can act without guessing.
Caution: I cannot mark the code merge-ready because I do not yet have the branch/diff/commit range or the implementation summary; without those, I cannot point to exact files, tests, or failing traces. The guidance below is therefore prescriptive but generic — you must attach the actual git range and affected files to make the review actionable.
Next Step: Provide the minimum required inputs: base SHA, head SHA (or PR link), a 2–3 sentence summary of intended behavior/requirements, and the top 5 changed files or directories. I will then produce a file-specific checklist and prioritized findings mapped to critical blockers versus follow-ups.

Detailed template to attach to the PR (apply after you share the git range and file list):

- Correctness
  Review goal: Verify the change implements the intended behavior and does not introduce regressions in existing logic.
  Required evidence: diff of changed files; unit test results; examples of input/output for new behavior; any API contract or acceptance criteria.
  Concrete checks: run unit test suite for affected modules (provide command, e.g., pytest path/to/changed); for each modified function, confirm behavior against acceptance criteria and edge cases.

- Architecture
  Review goal: Confirm design fits the system architecture, respects module boundaries, and avoids hidden coupling.
  Required evidence: component diagram or README if large change; list of new public interfaces; high-level rationale for design choices in PR description.
  Concrete checks: inspect dependency changes (package.json/go.mod/requirements.txt); ensure there are no direct imports violating boundaries and that new modules have clear responsibilities.

- Testing
  Review goal: Ensure adequate unit, integration, and regression coverage for changed behavior.
  Required evidence: test files added/updated; test coverage report (overall and per-file); CI run logs.
  Concrete checks: verify new behavior has positive and negative tests; confirm coverage for edge cases and error paths; run integration tests that exercise external interactions.

- Performance
  Review goal: Identify potential runtime or resource regressions under expected workloads.
  Required evidence: benchmarks or microbenchmarks for hotspots; complexity analysis for new algorithms; profiling traces if available.
  Concrete checks: for hot paths, run benchmark script or simple load test (provide command to run). If none exist, add a minimal benchmark that exercises the changed path.

- Production readiness
  Review goal: Confirm observability, error handling, deploy safety, and rollback paths are addressed.
  Required evidence: logging/metric changes, feature flag plan (if any), migration scripts with safety checks, deployment runbook notes, and any DB schema-change plans.
  Concrete checks: ensure new errors are logged with context, metrics are emitted for health and count, feature flags are included for risky changes, and migrations are backward-compatible or have rollback steps.

Use the next step above to provide the git range and top changed files so I can convert this template into a file-specific, prioritized review checklist separating critical blockers, important issues, and minor polish.

output:
Summary: Current review posture: Insufficiently grounded. I do not yet have the diff range, base/head SHAs, or a clear requirements/acceptance criteria document, so I cannot complete a comprehensive review. From the information provided, the task is to apply the Code Review Coordination Skill to the task branch, but no concrete artifacts were attached.
Action: Treat this as a triage step and collect the minimum evidence needed to complete a strong review:
- Provide the git base SHA and head SHA or a PR/branch link so reviewers can see the exact changes.
- Provide a short implementation summary (one paragraph) describing the intent and which acceptance criteria or design doc this change implements.
- List the highest-risk files or subsystems modified (or a short path/glob) and estimated impact (e.g., user-visible feature, background worker, DB migration).
- Attach current test evidence: unit/integration test names and results, CI run link, and any benchmark or load test results if performance-sensitive.
Caution: Do not assume merge-readiness. Without the concrete diff and acceptance criteria, any assessment would be speculative. Also avoid inventing findings: reviewers must rely on repository-level evidence (commits, CI, tests, and spec) to distinguish blockers from follow-ups.
Next Step: Please supply the git base and head SHAs (or PR/branch link), a short implementation intent statement, and the list of highest-risk files or components modified. With those, I will produce a focused review checklist that separates critical blockers from important follow-ups and lists exact evidence needed to close the review.

query.text: I am the end user. Please answer me directly and do not output any internal audit, replay, handoff, log, or internal_capsule content. output:
Summary: You asked for a code-review-ready coordination note for a change that spans many files and for which the implementer cannot yet identify the highest-risk areas. The immediate goal is to produce a clear, actionable review request that acknowledges the complication and focuses reviewer attention without requiring a full re-discovery of the diff.
Action: State the implementation scope (what was intended to change), the requirement source (ticket/PR description or design doc), and the exact git range (base sha → head sha or PR link). Explain that the breadth of files means we will triage risk by impact surface rather than file count: request the implementer to provide (1) a one-paragraph intent summary, (2) a short list of touched subsystems (e.g., auth, DB schema, API, worker), and (3) three candidate “highest-risk” flows or entry points they suspect. If they cannot pick three, ask for runtime telemetry, error logs, or test failures pointing to likely hotspots. Then ask for the parts of the diff the author believes are ready for immediate review (a focused subset or specific modules) so reviewers can start with the most likely-impact changes.
Caution: Because the change is broad and the highest-risk areas are unclear, do not conclude the code is review-complete or safe to merge. Broad diffs increase the chance of unintended coupling, schema mismatches, API contract regressions, and missing test coverage. Prioritize proving correctness on a small set of high-impact flows first; otherwise reviewers will need to do full-system reasoning which is slower and error-prone.
Next Step: Ask the implementer to provide: base and head shas (or PR link), a one-paragraph intent/requirements summary, the list of touched subsystems, and either (a) up to three highest-risk flows or (b) supporting evidence (telemetry, failing tests, or critical logs) that indicate where reviewers should focus. Assign the reviewer owner to start with those artifacts and the specified diff subset.

Summary: You want a pre-merge review coordination for a branch but have not provided a reliable base SHA or a clear requirements source. That missing context prevents a full, confident readiness assessment and narrows what reviewers can verify.
Action: I will treat the request as a targeted coordination note that (1) acknowledges the gap, (2) lists the minimum required evidence to continue, and (3) frames the review focus so reviewers can make a meaningful assessment despite the gap. Concretely: request the base SHA and head SHA (or a PR/branch link), a one-paragraph acceptance criteria or requirements pointer, and the highest-risk files or subsystems changed. While those are pending, prepare a short review checklist focused on correctness of logic, public API/backwards-compatibility, and production-safety guards that can be applied to the provided diff fragment or filenames.
Caution: Without a reliable base SHA or explicit requirements, we must not declare the change merge-ready. Missing git range or acceptance criteria increases the risk of reviewing the wrong comparison (e.g., rebased vs. intended base), overlooking regressions vs. intended behavior, and mis-prioritizing tests. Review findings done under this uncertainty should be treated as provisional: they may need revalidation once the correct base and acceptance criteria are provided.
Next Step: Provide the minimum inputs: base SHA (or the target branch name + repo), head SHA (or PR/branch link), and a brief acceptance criteria summary (one paragraph). If you can’t supply the full range, at minimum list the top 3 modified files or components so I can draft a focused pre-merge review checklist and a short list of concrete checks reviewers should perform.

The immediate goal is to prepare a handoff-ready review request that preserves that constraint and makes missing context explicit.
Action: 1) Confirm the implementation summary, requirements source, and exact diff range before any merge-readiness assessment. Request these minimum inputs now: base SHA, head SHA (or branch names with an exact commit range), a short requirements/acceptance-criteria summary or pointer (ticket/PR description), and the list of highest-risk files or components changed. 2) Classify the review focus upfront into correctness, architecture, testing, performance, and production readiness and note that no “ready to merge” verdict is permitted until the minimum inputs above are provided and verified. 3) Draft the review request for reviewers that (a) states the known change summary, (b) calls out missing inputs explicitly, and (c) lists concrete checks to perform once inputs are available (unit/integration test coverage, backward-compat checks, schema/migration review, safety toggles, rollout plan, and monitoring/alert expectations).
Caution: Do not infer or pretend coverage of any file-level changes, test outcomes, or requirement fulfillment without the provided diff range and acceptance criteria. Next Step: Provide the minimum missing evidence so the review can proceed: base SHA, head SHA (or exact branch/commit range), a one-paragraph acceptance criteria or requirements link, and the top 3 files or components you believe are highest risk. Once received, I will produce a handoff-ready review request that lists critical blockers, important follow-ups, and targeted reviewer checks.

Action: Acknowledge the complication: with many touched files, review effort and risk scale nonlinearly—broad diffs make it costly to find regressions and increase the chance of missing cross-cutting effects. To make the review effective now, provide a narrow, prioritized slice of evidence: 1) the exact commit range or branch name (base SHA and head SHA), 2) the list of top 5–8 files or modules you believe changed behavior most (or, if unknown, the files with the largest diffs or most runtime impact), and 3) a short statement of intended behavior / acceptance criteria for the feature or refactor. If you can’t pick files, provide automated outputs that direct reviewers (e.g., test failures, failing integration traces, CI job names, or a runtime error/stack trace tied to the change).
Caution: Do not treat broad coverage or passing unit tests as sufficient evidence of safety—when many files change, missing integration or runtime regressions is the primary risk. Until the commit range and the most-likely impact surface are confirmed, we cannot declare the code ready to merge and any high-level approval would be premature.
Next Step: Provide the commit range (base SHA → head SHA) plus either (A) the 5–8 highest-risk files/modules with a one-line reason each, or (B) if you can’t identify them, upload CI test results and any failing traces that narrow where runtime behavior changed. Assign an owner (implementer or lead reviewer) to supply that minimal evidence so the review can focus on correctness, integration risk, and production readiness.

Caution: Do not consider the change merge-ready until the git range and acceptance criteria are confirmed and tests covering the modified behaviors are available. Without the base/head SHAs and test evidence we must avoid asserting correctness or labeling issues as non-blocking — those judgments depend on concrete diffs and test outcomes.

Summary: You have a code change that spans many files and the high-risk areas aren’t yet summarized; the immediate goal is to prepare a clear, handoff-ready review request that reduces ambiguity and focuses reviewer effort on the true risks.
Action: Acknowledge the complication: broad, multi-file changes increase the chance of hidden coupling, untested code paths, and deployment/regression risks. To make the review actionable now, produce a minimal scoped packet: (1) a one-paragraph implementation summary describing intent and acceptance criteria, (2) the git base and head shas or PR link, (3) a short list (3–6) of candidate high-risk modules or file globs you suspect touch critical paths, and (4) representative diffs or sample files (the 5–10 files you believe are most relevant). If you can’t identify the top files, run a simple diff-file-frequency check (largest diffs, files touched by multiple commits, or files with public APIs) and attach the output. Also include current test-run results (unit/integration) and any failing tests.
Caution: Do not declare the change merge-ready until the git range, acceptance criteria, and either targeted review of the high-risk files or test evidence are provided. Broad changes can conceal architectural regressions, permission/security gaps, or performance regressions; without a narrowed scope or concrete test traces, reviewers will be forced to either (a) do a costly full repo pass or (b) mark the PR blocked for missing context.
Next Step: Owner (implementer): supply the base and head shas (or PR link), a 2–3 sentence acceptance criteria list, and either (A) your top 5–10 files/globs you believe are highest risk, or (B) the diff-file-frequency output if you’re unsure — plus current test results. With that minimal packet a reviewer can triage critical blockers vs. follow-ups and proceed to a focused correctness and production-readiness review.

Action: I'll treat this as a coordination request: identify the implementation intent, scope the exact diff to review once provided, and prepare a focused checklist reviewers can use to evaluate correctness, tests, and production readiness. Right now the actionable path is to confirm the minimum Git range (base SHA and head SHA or branch names), the acceptance criteria or ticket/PR description that defines intended behavior, and the highest-risk files or components touched.
Caution: Without a confirmed base SHA and a clear requirements source we cannot (and must not) claim the change is ready to merge. Missing those items creates three concrete risks: (1) reviewers may assess the wrong diff or miss unrelated commits; (2) acceptance criteria won't map to implementation so behavioral gaps can be hidden; (3) test coverage and regression impact cannot be scoped reliably. These are blockers for a production-ready review.
Next Step: Provide the minimum required inputs: base SHA (or the base branch name and the commit it should start from), head SHA (or the PR branch), and a short requirements summary or ticket link with explicit acceptance criteria. Also list 3–5 highest-risk files or modules changed. Once you supply those, I'll return a handoff-ready review request that separates critical blockers from follow-ups and lists exact reviewer checks.

output:
Summary: The branch change needs a production-ready review request, but key context is missing: the implementation scope, the git range (base and head SHAs or PR), and the requirements source. Because those are unclear, we cannot conclude the code is merge-ready.
Action: Prepare a minimal, handoff-ready review package that includes (1) a one-line implementation summary (what was changed and why), (2) the authoritative requirements or acceptance criteria (link or short text), and (3) the exact review target: base SHA and head SHA or a PR number and branch name. Also list the highest-risk files or components touched (top 3–5) and any available test evidence (unit/integration test names, CI run links, and failing/passing status).
Caution: Do not mark the change as ready to merge or close the review loop until the git range and requirements are confirmed. Without those, reviewers cannot reliably assess correctness, scope creep, or whether tests cover required behavior — claiming readiness would violate the review policy and risks shipping regressions.
Next Step: Provide the missing minimum inputs now: base SHA, head SHA (or PR URL), a brief statement of the acceptance criteria or requirement doc link, and the 3 highest-risk files or modules changed. Once provided, I will produce a focused review request that separates critical blockers from follow-ups and lists exact reviewer checks.

output:
Summary: You asked for a review coordination note for a task branch, with the explicit constraint that I must not invent reviewer findings, test results, or file-level evidence that hasn't been provided or verified. The immediate goal is to prepare a handoff-ready review request that documents what is known, what is missing, and the minimal concrete next actions so a reviewer can proceed without assuming unverified facts.
Action: 1) Confirmed implementation summary required: ask the implementer to provide a one-paragraph summary of what was changed and why (linking to the acceptance criteria or ticket).
2) Confirm the review target: request the base SHA and head SHA (or PR/branch URL) and the exact commit range to review.
3) Provide a focused review scope template the implementer should fill in (so reviewers don't infer unprovided evidence):
   - Changed modules/files: list the top-level packages or files expected to contain the substantive logic changes.
   - Intended behavior / acceptance criteria: list explicit conditions the change must satisfy in production.
   - Tests included: enumerate unit/integration/e2e tests added or updated and their locations.
   - Performance/scale concerns: note any hotspots or expected workload changes.
   - Backwards-compatibility and DB/migration impacts: list migrations or schema changes and rollout strategy.
4) Ask for concrete artifacts to attach: the diff/PR link, CI run links (pass/fail and failing tests if any), unit-test coverage summary, and any benchmark traces or logs that motivated the change.
5) Provide an explicit reviewer checklist to use (no invented findings):
   - Correctness: verify logic vs. acceptance criteria and edge cases identified by implementer.
   - Architecture & design: confirm API boundaries and invariants claimed by the implementer.
   - Tests: confirm tests cover the listed acceptance criteria and add missing tests where gaps exist.
   - Production readiness: confirm migrations, feature flags, observability (metrics/logs/alerts), and rollback plan are present.
   - Security/privacy: confirm any data handling or auth changes are reviewed.
6) State a merge gating rule: do not merge until base/head are confirmed, CI green for the specified commit range, and acceptable test coverage for changed behavior has been attached.
Caution: I will not and must not fabricate any file-level findings, test outcomes, or CI results. Until you provide the commit range, PR/diff, and the explicit list of changed files/tests/acceptance criteria, I cannot mark any item as verified or say the code is ready to merge. Any reviewer guidance below assumes the implementer supplies the requested artifacts; otherwise the review must be limited to high-level architecture and the stated acceptance criteria only.
Next Step: Please provide the minimum set of inputs: base SHA, head SHA (or PR/branch URL), a 1–2 sentence implementation summary, the list of highest-risk files or modules changed, and links to CI run(s) and any new/changed tests. Once those are provided I will produce a handoff-ready review request that separates critical blockers from follow-ups and lists concrete reviewer actions.
